request.question: What acress starred in both "Third Person" and "From Here to Eternity"?

title: Grammatical person
Direct–inverse language
Third Person (band)
Kim Basinger
Du-reformen
Third Person (film)
Pitcairn's Island (novel)
Note verbale
Shinji Mikami
Appears
sentences: Grammatical person, in linguistics, is the grammatical distinction between deictic references to participant(s) in an event; typically the distinction is between the speaker (first person), the addressee (second person), and others (third person).

Put in simple colloquial English, first person is that which includes the speaker, namely, "I," "we," "me," and "us," second person is the person or people to whom are spoken, literally, "you," and third person includes all that is not listed above.
Grammatical person typically defines a language's set of personal pronouns.
It also frequently affects verbs, and sometimes nouns or possessive relationships.

The definition of a direct–inverse language is a matter under research, but it is widely understood to involve different grammar for transitive predications according to the relative positions of their "subject" and their "object" on a "person hierarchy", which, in turn, is some combination of saliency and animacy specific to a given language.
The direct construction is the unmarked one.
The direct construction is used when the subject of the transitive clause outranks the object in the person hierarchy, and the inverse is used when the object outranks the subject.
The existence of direct–inverse morphosyntax is usually accompanied by proximate–obviative morphosyntax.
The direct–inverse dimension subsumes the proximate–obviative dimension.

Across languages, obviation almost always involves the third person (although second-person obviation is reported for some Nilo-Saharan languages ), and the direct–inverse alternation is usually presented as being a way of marking the proximate–obviative distinction between two (or more) third person arguments of a sentence.

However, there are at least two languages with inverse systems, the Mesoamerican languages Zoque and Huastec, in which inverse morphosyntax is never used when both subject and object are third person, but only when one of these arguments is third person and the other is a speech act participant (SAP), the first or second person .
Third Person was an improvising trio formed in 1990 in New York City, led by cellist Tom Cora and drummer Samm Bennett.
Each performance featured an invited guest: A third person.
Guests included guitarist Marc Ribot, harpist Zeena Parkins, clarinet player Don Byron and saxophone players George Cartwright and Kazutoki Umezu and many others.
Kimila Ann "Kim" Basinger ( ; born December 8, 1953) is an American actress, singer and former fashion model.
Following a successful modeling career in New York during the 1970s, Basinger moved to Los Angeles where she began her acting career on television in 1976.
She starred in several made-for-TV films, including a remake of "From Here to Eternity" (1979), before making her feature debut in the 1981 drama "Hard Country".
She won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress for her performance as Lynn Bracken in the 1997 film "L.A. Confidential".
Du-reformen (] , "the you-reform") was the process in the late 1960s of popularization of the second-person singular pronoun "du" as a universal form of address in Sweden.

The use of "du" (cognate with English "thou", French "tu", and German "du") replaced an intricate former system where people chiefly addressed each other in third person, with or without a preceding Mr./Mrs./Ms. "(herr, fru "or" fröken)" before the title, often omitting both surname and given name.

Less respectfully, people could be addressed with Mr./Mrs./Ms. plus surname, or in a family setting, alternatively even less respectfully, with the plain name or third person pronoun "han" ("he") or "hon" (she).

Third Person is a 2013 British-German-American romance film directed and written by Paul Haggis and starring an ensemble cast consisting of Liam Neeson, Mila Kunis, Adrien Brody, Olivia Wilde, James Franco, Moran Atias, Kim Basinger, and Maria Bello.
The film premiered at the 2013 Toronto Film Festival.
Pitcairn's Island is the third installment in the fictional trilogy by Charles Nordhoff and James Norman Hall about the mutiny aboard HMS "Bounty".
It is preceded by "Mutiny on the "Bounty"" and "Men Against the Sea".
The novel first appeared in "The Saturday Evening Post" (from 22 September 1934 through 3 November 1934) then was published in 1934 by Little, Brown and Company.
Chapters I–XV are told in the third person, and Chapters XVI–XXI are told in the first person by John Adams.
The epilogue that follows is in the third person.

Note verbale (] , verbal note) is a piece of diplomatic correspondence prepared in the third person and unsigned: less formal than a note (also called a letter of protest) but more formal than an aide-mémoire.
A note verbale can also be referred to as a third person note (TPN).
Shinji Mikami (Japanese: 三上 真司 , Hepburn: Mikami Shinji , born August 11, 1965) is a Japanese video game director and producer.
Starting his career at Capcom in 1990, he went on to direct many of the company's biggest titles.
He directed the first installment of the survival horror series "Resident Evil" in 1996 and the first installment of "Dino Crisis" in 1999.
He returned to Resident Evil to direct the remake of the first game in 2002.
He returned to direct third person shooter "Resident Evil 4" in 2005.
The last game he directed at Capcom was 2006's God Hand, a third person brawler.
He then worked with PlatinumGames to direct the third person shooter "Vanquish" in 2010.
After that he started his own studio called Tango Gameworks.
Under that company he directed the third person horror game "The Evil Within".
The company has since been acquired by the American company ZeniMax Media.
"Appears" is a song recorded by Japanese recording artist Ayumi Hamasaki.
It was released by Avex Trax on November 10, 1999 as the sixth single from her second studio album "Loveppears" (1999), which was released on the same day.
Alongside this, it has been released in several other territories with different release dates under her Western alias Ayu.
It also served as Hamasaki's first limited edition single, limiting physical sales to 300,000 copies.
The track was written by Hamasaki herself, while production was handled by long-time collaborator Max Matsuura.
Musically, "Appears" is a dance song written in third person perspective, and is about the third person watching a happy and loving relationship.
Upon its release, "Appears" received positive reviews from music critics.
Alexey Eremenko, writing for AllMusic, selected the track as the best song from the album and her career.
However, an editor from "CD Journal" criticized the amount of remixes on the CD single.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What acress starred in both "Third Person" and "From Here to Eternity"?
hop1_docs: From Here to Eternity (Envy album) | From Here To Eternity is the second album by the band Envy.  It was released in Japan in 1998 by the label H.G. Fact in both CD and LP formats.  As has been noted by reviewers, this album shows a logical progression from the previous one, and indicates to the listener which direction the band will take their sound in the future.

From Here to Eternity: Live | From Here to Eternity: Live is a live album by English punk rock band The Clash.  It was released on 4 October 1999 through Epic Records.  The songs were recorded at different shows.  Some of the recordings featured also appear in the film "Rude Boy".  "London's Burning", "What's My Name" and "I Fought the Law" were instrumentally overdubbed to repair some technical deficiencies of the original live recording.
From Here to Eternity (disambiguation) | From Here to Eternity is a 1953 film.
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder song) | "From Here to Eternity" is a song by songwriter, producer and performer Giorgio Moroder, released in 1977.

From Here to Eternity (miniseries) | From Here to Eternity is a 1979 American three-part, six-hour television miniseries and a remake of the 1953 film "From Here to Eternity" based on the 1951 novel of the same name.  All three conclude with the December 7, 1941 attack on Pearl Harbor.  The miniseries originally aired in three two-hour installments on three consecutive Wednesdays on NBC on February 14, 21 and 28, 1979.
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder album) | From Here to Eternity is a 1977 album composed, produced and performed by Italian musician Giorgio Moroder.

From Here to Eternity (Iron Maiden song) | "From Here to Eternity" is a song by English heavy metal band Iron Maiden, released as the second single from their album "Fear of the Dark", released in 1992.
hop1_titles: From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder song)
From Here to Eternity (Giorgio Moroder album)
From Here to Eternity (Iron Maiden song)
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - "Third Person": not mentioned in the provided passages.
  - "From Here to Eternity" (1953 film): passage 3 — "From Here to Eternity is a 1953 film."
  - "From Here to Eternity" (1979 miniseries): passage 5 — "From Here to Eternity is a 1979 ... television miniseries and a remake of the 1953 film."

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Which "From Here to Eternity" is intended (1953 film vs. 1979 miniseries) is unclear.
  - No cast or starring credits are present for either "From Here to Eternity" entry in the passages.
  - "Third Person" (the work referenced in the question) is missing entirely from the passages.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Full cast/starring list for "Third Person" (identify actresses credited).
  - Cast/starring list for the relevant "From Here to Eternity" version (1953 film and/or 1979 miniseries).
  - Confirmation that the same actress appears in both cast lists.

- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. "Third Person (film) cast" — retrieve the film page or cast list (e.g., Third Person (2013 film) — cast).
  2. "From Here to Eternity (1953 film) — cast" — retrieve the 1953 film page with starring credits.
  3. "From Here to Eternity (1979 miniseries) — cast" — retrieve the miniseries page with starring credits (in case the miniseries is intended).
  4. If quick search needed: query "actress who starred in both 'Third Person' and 'From Here to Eternity'".
hop2_docs: Third Person (film) | Third Person is a 2013 British-German-American romance film directed and written by Paul Haggis and starring an ensemble cast consisting of Liam Neeson, Mila Kunis, Adrien Brody, Olivia Wilde, James Franco, Moran Atias, Kim Basinger, and Maria Bello.  The film premiered at the 2013 Toronto Film Festival.

The Hangover Part III | The Hangover Part III is a 2013 American comedy film produced by Legendary Pictures and distributed by Warner Bros.  Pictures.  It is the third and final installment in "The Hangover" trilogy.  The film stars Bradley Cooper, Ed Helms, Zach Galifianakis, Justin Bartha, and Ken Jeong.  The supporting cast includes Jeffrey Tambor, Heather Graham, Mike Epps, Melissa McCarthy and John Goodman with Todd Phillips directing a screenplay written by himself and Craig Mazin.  The film follows the "Wolfpack" (Phil, Stu, Doug, and Alan) as they try to get Alan the help he needs after facing a personal crisis.  However, things go awry when an incident from the original film comes back to haunt them.

Google Cast | Google Cast, branded for consumer devices as Chromecast built-in, is a proprietary protocol developed by Google that enables mobile devices and personal computers to initiate and control playback of Internet-streamed audio/video content on a compatible device, such as a digital media player connected to a high-definition television or home audio system.  The protocol was first launched on July 24, 2013, to support Google's first-generation Chromecast player.  The Google Cast SDK was released on February 3, 2014, allowing third parties to modify their software to support the protocol.  According to Google, over 20,000 Google Cast-ready apps were available as of May 2015.  Google Cast would later be built into the Nexus Player and other Android TV devices, such as televisions, as well as soundbars, speakers, and subsequent Chromecast players.

Killing Jesus (film) | Killing Jesus is an American television film inspired by the 2013 book of the same title by Bill O'Reilly and Martin Dugard.  This is National Geographic's third installment of television adaptations of O'Reilly's non-fiction books which included "Killing Lincoln" and "Killing Kennedy".  The cast includes Haaz Sleiman, Kelsey Grammer, Stephen Moyer, Emmanuelle Chriqui, and John Rhys-Davies.

Tasha Marbury | LaTasha Marbury (January 11, 1976) is an American reality show personality and cast member of VH1's reality series "Basketball Wives".  She was a member of the season 5 cast which premiered August 19, 2013.

Luke Mitchell | Luke Mitchell (born 17 April 1985) is an Australian actor and model.  He attended the Film and Television Studio International and won the role of Chris Knight in "Neighbours".  Mitchell appeared in the third season of "" as Will, before starring as Romeo Smith in "Home and Away".  The role saw Mitchell win the Most Popular New Male Talent Logie Award in 2010.  In 2013 he was cast as John Young in the CW's "The Tomorrow People," whom he played until the series cancellation.  In 2015, he joined the cast of "Marvel's Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D." as the Inhuman Lincoln Campbell as a recurring character for its second season before being promoted to series regular for the third season.

Ferne McCann | Ferne Alice McCann (born 6 August 1990) is an English television personality and presenter.  She is best known as a cast member of the ITVBe reality show "The Only Way Is Essex", having been on the show since the ninth series in 2013 until 2016.  In 2015, she participated in the fifteenth series of "I'm a Celebrity...Get Me Out of Here! " and finished in third place.  In early 2016, she became a regular showbiz reporter on "This Morning".
response.summary_2: Passage 1 identifies Third Person's cast (including Mila Kunis, Olivia Wilde, Moran Atias, Kim Basinger, and Maria Bello), but the provided passages do not contain cast information for either the 1953 film or the 1979 miniseries From Here to Eternity, so no actress appearing in both can be determined from these sources.
response.answer: No actress can be determined from the provided summaries.
